title: vimeo_1202354246
displayTitle: 2026-06-18 - Environment Committee - 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment
topic: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment
description: Thursday 18 June 2026 | Environment Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Hearing of Evidence: Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Rt Hon Simon Upton Ministry for the Environment Sam Buckle, Acting Secretary for the Environment Anne Haira, Deputy Secretary (Partnerships, Investment and Enablement)
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment. Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment. Rt Hon Simon Upton

chunks.text: Welcome, Commissioner and team, to the select committee this afternoon. You have 30 minutes, half an hour with us. I see you have a presentation. Yes. So we will hand over to you to talk us through that. And then we'll open up for questions. Thank you. Yes, thank you. It'll be pretty brief.

We thought it wouldn't be a proper presentation if we didn't have some slides. I'll just quickly take you through a few interesting facts and figures, and then you can ask whatever you like. This little slide here shows you how our work mix has changed. An investigation is a big report, doorstop size.

Mm-hmm. And that's the blue. And responding to public concerns are those green ones. There's lots of them, but they're little weeny things. And then the dark green is responses to public policy. So that is actually making submissions on government documents or coming along here and talking to you about select committee stuff.

So you can see there's been a big increase in the amount of government driven, both legislation and policy documents and so on and so forth. And what that has meant actually is that we are doing less in the way of investigations because we don't have the resources to do both.

So our work mix is changing. You'd expect that. There's nothing strange about that. It's probably happened in the past as well. This next slide I thought might interest you. I think I'm one of the few bits of the public sector which can tell you precisely what our direct costs are for our work.

We're set up to do this, so I know how much public money I'm spending on each of these big chunks of work anyhow. So you can read that for yourself. But the one we did on the drivers of afforestation in New Zealand, which was all about the ETS and so on and so forth, that was- Mm-hmm... a thick end of $1 million. Whereas writing a little note on the contribution of historical methane emissions to present day warming was $12,000.

This is staff time, and it is externally contracted work. So, we don't have a large external budget, because we try to employ a pretty multidisciplinary range of skills. So there's usually somebody on the team who knows enough to do a lot of the work, but if we want to actually get something very precise, we want some modeling done, or we want to actually commission some research, then we spend money outside.

But the bulk of that is our own staff time. But I think it's really useful and interesting. I have to say, I used to know this stuff when I was Minister for the Environment eons years ago. At the ministry, we had a very fine-grained output purchasing system. It just seems to have evaporated. And I found it very useful, and I do now, because if you say, "Oh, well, why don't we just go and do X?"

You've got a sense of how much resource. It's very easy to say to staff, "Oh, just do more" or, "Just add in," but there are limits. This actually gives you some sense of how much you've got tied up. And also, afterwards, you can say, well, actually, was it really worth spending so much in terms of resources and time on a thing? So I personally think this is really good.

Now, there's something which is not up there, which will be particularly interesting to you people on this committee, and that is our estimate of direct staff costs associated with giving you advice on the planning bill and the natural environment bill between the 1st of January and the 30th of April, comes out at $160,222.

That's the staff cost. So, you know that you had a budget for using Rowan Carruthers KCs, who's far grander than me and charges an enormous rate. Here, we're just a little office, but we've kept a track of staff time. We've diverted $166,000 worth of staff cost to helping the committee with that single legislative endeavor. So it just gives you a sense, actually, of when you ask someone to do something, it does cost.

We try to track that, so you've got a sense of how we're using our resources. That's very helpful. The next slides are work that's underway or coming up. I can be very quick, and then I suspect you probably want to ask questions. This one is coming a bit over a month away It won't have that title, I suspect, but it says Technologies to Unlock Environmental Information. As you know, I've been working on this topic for the last decade almost, the quality of New Zealand's environmental information, banging my head against brick walls and ministerial doors and all sorts of things

to try to get some attention, and the last budget has had some quite positive things to say on this area. But I've got more to say, and what we've done is tried to pull together the findings of the last nine years really to say, "Okay, if you want to put it all in one place, this is what the Parliamentary Commission for the Environment thinks you need to do if you want to take this stuff seriously."

And the bit which is new in this report, and it really is, I think it's interesting, is just looking at some of the new technologies which are available. A lot of these are remote sensing from space and so on, which can change the game, because we'll never get what we need using old technology. So, the use of big data sets, the use of modeling, the use of remote sensing, this actually outlines some of the stuff, and it gives you three really nice little case studies. We'll bring the report to you, but it goes through three bits

of New Zealand. Yours is one, Hawke's Bay. Right. What data have we got and what haven't we got? Just to get a sense of the... It's a bit, you could either say it's moth-eaten or it's like Gruyère cheese. We know some stuff, and we don't know other stuff. So this is putting the facts as we know them in front of you. So that's going to come out in the last week in July. Mm-hmm. Is that right? Yeah.

Yeah. So you'll get that. Well, I mean, you'll have to find some time for a hearing, but I hope you do. It's very relevant to the spatial planning and the national environment bills that you're looking at. Very relevant, and there is one recommendation that relates to the new planning system in it. So, I'm going to be producing this report before the election on what's the future of the New Zealand Emissions Trading Scheme, with a particular focus on afforestation and offsetting.

And again, this is pulling together a lot of work I've done over the last five or six years. There is some new modeling in this, which we've commissioned and worked on. In short, the ETS has real problems, and forestry is at the heart of them, and somebody is going to have to address it someday. And the longer you leave it, the more difficult it will be. So that's what that report's about.

The next one you'll be familiar with. Each year after the budget, we then start pulling it all apart. The famous graph. Yes. Famous. The Sankey diagram. Yes. So you'll have this- Sankey ... before your scrutiny. Actually, I don't know what happens in an election year, do you? Because normally it's about sort of November, December, isn't it? December.

It is December, yeah. Yeah, December. So anyhow, we'll have the latest edition of this for you to pull apart. And again, I would suggest to you that rather than just looking at votes, you have a look at choose an outcome or choose part of an outcome, and look at all the agencies that are contributing. And I mean, the last budget's had some really interesting things in it, like the wildings stuff. Now, it's quite a big slug of money there. Interesting question to ask about that, in relation to other things, right, is that too much given that you've only got so much for that outcome?

Or is it still too little? I mean, it's not to point the finger. It's simply to get a sense of priority and where resources are going. That's what your job is, and I've made it my job to help you ask those questions. There's also a report which will probably, I would think it'll come out early next year.

We reviewed the terrestrial indigenous biosecurity management system. That was the weeds report. We're doing the same for offshore- Mm-hmm ... for the marine environment. So, it'll be the same sort of report. It'll say, "Okay, these are the issues." We've got some interesting original research on biosecurity risk assessment.

Here's the state of things. This is the way MPI is going about it, and could it be done better? I have to tell you, this is much harder stuff than stuff on land. It would be. Mm-hmm. So I doubt whether we'll be saying as much actually. But it'll be interesting. So that one's coming down the track. Then this is a really big one. This is for next year.

Resource efficiency of the New Zealand economy. This goes back at least a couple of years, and we will have spent a lot of money on this, by the time we finish it. But you will be aware, you're always hearing people saying, "Oh, we need to have zero waste" or circular economy and all of this stuff. There's a lot of aspirational stuff around this. There is. I think people frequently have no idea what they are saying when they make these claims.

And so what I wanted to do was to understand just what the resource flows and the pollution flows from those resource flows in our economy look like. And, we've put out a report last year, which gave an overview of New Zealand's current natural resource use profile.

You've got that. So that was an early piece of work. What we're going to be focusing on now is how resource use and waste generation might evolve over the coming decades. And we're not just looking at the New Zealand economy, we're looking at New Zealand and the global economy. So we can track resource use in terms of imports and in terms of our exports, but also the environmental footprint of the imports because a lot of the time we say we're all clean and green, but we've just offshored it.

And also the- Our environment down to provide the goods we provide. So it's a reasonably sophisticated piece of work. We've been working with the people who do the GTAP models at Purdue University in the States, to commission some of the underlying input for this.

This is a thing which a lot of governments do. I think the New Zealand government should've done this years ago. It doesn't. So we've done it. But I think it will be as good as anything anyone's done in the world at a national level. And if you've got this information and you're prepared to then... We've done the methodology, so to speak.

If you'd like to carry this forward, this should be a tool that MB or M-cert or whatever it is, they should be actually doing this stuff. Because if you want to ask questions about resource flows in the New Zealand economy, this is absolutely vital information, and we have not had it up until now. So anyway. And it's useful not just for environmental stuff- No... but for supply chain and- Yeah... that sort of stuff.

Can I just ask a question? How are you going to get proprietary information, like information held by private companies to put into this? There are global- Yeah... input output tables that- Yeah... you can't think that this stuff is accurate to a decimal point. No. No. But it does give you a pretty accurate overview of the flow of resources.

So it's going to be open source data, is it? Yeah. Yeah. Absolutely. Yeah. And there's a ton of the stuff. Yeah. There's a ton of the stuff, it's just not being analyzed in a way which can provide these sort of answers. But as Jeff says, on the supply chain side, it's really interesting. We know where we get stuff from, we know who buys from us, what's the overall picture?

So- Can I just add something there? Yeah, go for it. So, when you're making a widget you can have an environmental footprint, which is what this is measuring, but your widget still probably is still got a lower footprint than potentially is made anywhere in the world. So bringing that into the report as well to make sure-... we've got context of actually- Yeah, absolutely. Yeah... it's not just the imp- it's not- Yeah. No. And this is the thing, what it will show, I think, is that some of the stuff we import has a very heavy footprint.

If you did it here, it might be lower or vice versa. The whole point is this is not about trying to hone in on a problem, it's to say, "Look, this is fundamental data which should be in the public arena." We're putting it together, so then if this committee or if a government department or a minister wanted to do to ask some questions, you have a methodology for doing it. So anyhow, I'm sorry, you'll have to wait till next year for that one Then just quickly some research notes that we're going to be doing.

Because mining is on the up in terms of fast-track applications and so forth, we thought we'd just find out what we knew about what best practice looked like if you were going to be proposing to mine, and what sort of conditions would be placed on mine operators, particularly thinking of the long term after it's closed and all the rest of it. So we've commissioned some international work to try to get a sense of that and, once we've finished peer reviewing that and digesting it, we'll make that available. So again, it's a bit of a resource which will be available to

panels, it'll be available to the government. We've just tried to say, "Well, this is what we know about best practice." When? What's the timing on that? That should be public in between those two other reports, early August, I would think. Okay. Right. So it's quite close. Second one, if you want to click on is, I'll let you talk about this because it's very much your patch.

Yeah. Monetizing ecosystems. Again, this was sort of prompted by the sorts of conversations that are being thrown up by fast-track about how you come up with net benefits, a net benefit test for a lot of these economic development projects. And there's a lot of discussion about how you factor in the value of the environment into those sorts of conversations.

And so what we're doing is updating the work. There's only really been one big study done on this in New Zealand. It was done in 2013, and it was very rudimentary at the time, and we are updating that for the latest thinking on this. It doesn't help you think about the value of very rare or endangered ecosystems, but it is a tool that can be used to think about the value of ecosystem services, when you're trading them off for- Yeah... say, mining or infrastructure projects writ large. Yeah.

And why did you choose that photo? And do you think there's a fast-track mining project up for trade? Don't worry. You've got- No, I've got a bit of a penchant for botanical gardens. All right. So Okay, taking out- A member for Dunedin was concerned. Yes. That makes you nervous. Don't read anything into that. Then you may recall before the last election, there were all sorts of claims being made about the oil and gas ban, and I just produced a little note saying, "Well, will the sky fall in economically, and will the sky

fall in..." Or, sorry, "Will this make the planet safe environmentally?" Because I really thought it was a very uninformed sort of debate, and so we came up with a note which said, "Well, actually, look, it's really not such a big deal economically, and by the way, environmentally it isn't a particularly big deal either." The government no doubt has its reasons for doing that.

I'm looking at doing just an update to put some of the facts on the table, in the hope that As you all have these debates, it'll be a useful resource to you. Again, it's not taking a policy position, it's just updating those facts and saying what we know about them. All right? And then finally, here, and this I do hope the committee takes an interest in, the science reforms, as you know, have now well advanced and we've seen how much money's been given to each of the four pillars, and I've put everyone on notice that you can move the money around. That's fine.

I will be tracking the extent to which public money is being taken away from the environment and put into other pillars, and I will be tracking the consequences both for research output and for the human capital base, because I really am concerned. I have to say, the environment, there is a reduction planned in environmental research because it's all been done off a zero baseline, which does surprise me.

I'd have thought that if this is one area where really the country should be investing more, but my view is my view. But certainly, the reduction in environment is not enormous. But when you are spending as little as we are, even small reductions can have a significant impact. Tracking that is not easy, and in fairness to the government's reallocation of resources, this high tech pillar, which they're putting the lion's share of the reallocation to, some of that will probably be spent in technologies which are being used in the

environment, and that's one of the things my report to you in a month's time is going to say. Look, we really need to build our technical skill base on measuring stuff. So it's not a straightforward and easy thing, but I am working closely with MB, who've been very helpful, I have to say, in trying to track where money is currently being spent and where it'll be spent in the future. I can tell you now that the new system will, once it finally settles down, be better than where we were because right now, MB cannot tell you until after the

event where quite a lot of the money gets spent. And in my view, that's not ideal. I think as a parliament, you should know where the priorities are and where the money is going in relatively real time, and it is possible to do that. And that's the best thing about the reform, which I think once it plays out, we'll have better information. If it shows there's a reduction in environmental research, I will be able to track that through that Sankey diagram to tell you which bits of environmental research are most affected.

Because it could be that there's increases in some areas, but there's reductions in others. So, again, it's just a piece of accounting tracking. This is the committee which should take an interest in that, and I think that is it. Is it? We've got one last slide on potential investigations.

Oh, sorry. Well, yeah, I won't spend too long on this because we're still thinking about this. Once we've completed the piece of work on resource efficiency, then okay, all right, well, that's nice. What do you do with that? And one of the things we could do is look sectorally at the New Zealand economy and ask the same questions, but in a more applied way.

I'm particularly interested in this food security one. I wouldn't have thought this was an issue for a country like New Zealand, but you talk to, as I did, some of the arable people in Canterbury and the fact that you've got some imported commodities effectively putting them out of business. Now, fine, it's the cheapest if it comes from Oz, except when the supply chains get disrupted and suddenly you can't put that back together because you've lost the skills, you've lost the IP in some cases. So there's interesting issues right through the economy.

I'm thinking about food security as one. And the other one is, you'd be aware that DOC has got this bioinvest tool, to try to decide which moth you save next sort of thing. And it's really quite a sophisticated piece of work. I really want to review that and ask what are the sort of trade-offs we're making.

Do we know enough to even know whether we're meeting conservation goals? How would we actually go about that? So we haven't done any detailed thinking about either of those, but those are the next big investigations I hope we can get off the block next year. So there we are. That's what we're doing. That's how we're spending your four odd million dollars.

And it is the smallest, I think it's just about the smallest appropriation on the books. So we're very careful with it. You certainly do a lot with it. But hey- Yeah... thank you very much, Simon. Appreciate that presentation. Look, we'll open up to questions now. And Celia has had her hand up online-... for quite some time, so we will go to Celia first.

I should have introduced Helen, by the way- Hi, Helen... who's the chief ops officer, and I think you know Jeff, who's my chief policy guy. Brilliant. Celia. Thank you, and kia ora. One of my questions about whether you'd be looking at bioinvest, you beautifully answered in the last slide, so that's great. I did have a couple of questions about the environment spend, and then one question about your targets. So with the environment spend, and I've probably asked you this before, is there any work going on seeing how much councils put

in as well as government agencies, or do you feel that that is outside your remit? We are actually doing a pilot- Yeah... which will be in this year's estimate of environmental expenditure, which looks at exactly that issue. It's not all beer and skittles, frankly.

It's not easy to do. But we have given it a crack for a few councils for one year, and so you'll see the results of that. I think the key issue going forward is around the council reform process. Yeah. That's really why we didn't continue the work any further because, uh- Yeah. They're just up to their ears. This is regional councils we're working with.

Okay? Because they are the main spenders on the environment as distinct from other types of planning. So they're the ones that we need to talk to. Well, I have to say that you might revise that when you think about Predator Free Wellington. It's certainly the city council- Yeah... an equal player, but there we are.

A minor detail in the scheme of things. My question, if the chair will allow me to ask one and then I'll shut up, is the targets you've got about the number of recommendations that are adopted. Now, unless you're going to provide a whole lot of really wishy-washy targets- ... that, which I hope you don't do, that is really fairly much out of your control.

Yes. And I don't know that it's... It's quite interesting, in my view, it shows which governments are taking any notice and which aren't, but I'm not sure that's your job to- No, we don't have control. No, and we've talked about this every year. We do this because the Audit Office says we have to, basically. Well, let's try not doing it. I mean- No, no. Oh, no, no, no.

We have a very good relationship with the Auditor's Office. It's fine. Look, it's fine to... I think we should track, I think we really should track the extent to which our recommendations are picked up. Okay. But it's not within our hands, as you say. And the other thing is that recommendations might not be picked up in year one or year two, or even in year three, but over time, the ideas are socialized, and it's a resource which gets drawn on.

So, we do actually have a reasonable hit rate. The money that the government is spending in this year's budget, adding to the information base, is not unrelated to the fact that I have been hammering this point for the last six or seven years. So eventually, things do find their way into the system. But equally, I think you're right, Celia, it would be just crazy if I came up with recommendations to try to hit some target. It could be a sign that I'm really on the money, only 10% were picked up because, in fact, they are so

difficult, people are having a lot of trouble digesting them. Thank you very much, and thank you for focusing on the big issues. And a very cheeky last question, you should be leading MCERT, and then we'll all be happy. Oh, I see. Okay. I'm not sure that appeals. Rachel, a supplementary.

Yeah. You're still on this proportion of your recommendations that are adopted. How do you track things like the data one that you just said? Because you have been- Banging on... I think you said banging on. Yeah. And I'm not going to disagree-... with that turn of phrase at all. How do you track those things that you've been saying repetitively?

Yeah. But also, how do you track the things that you might have done a report about some years ago that is- You need to come over and see Sean's spreadsheet- Oh, okay... which is meticulous. Yeah. We have somebody called Sean. Sean. I think Sean might have been born in the PCE's office. His institutional memory exceeds anything else in the public sector in the space, literally.

He goes back a very long way. He goes back three commissioners. Yeah. And he honestly has all of those recommendations, and he tracks them. Fantastic. That's great. Great to hear about Sean. Can I ask- Yeah, yeah... another question on that? And so on your analysis, on Sean's analysis, do you look at how you are influential in terms of, I know you've changed over the years some of your reports. You'll do smaller summaries of them, and that was from feedback from select committees, and you bang on about things for years and years, and you talk to very

many different people. Are there other ways you think, or is there a best way to try and get things into our political heads, to ask the right questions in opposition and to make the right budget choices and other things in government? We're really outside my role.

My role's set in an act, as you know. By the way, I just read the report of the Offices of Parliament, a select committee on Offices of Parliament from 1988. Mm-hmm. And this included names like the Honorable Ruth Richardson and Mr. Murray McClee, and the Honorable Trevor Klean.

And the chair was not Bill Sutton, the other Sutton from your side. But they all said- Jim. Jim Sutton. Jim Sutton, that's right. From Ōmaru. Yeah. Yes, that's right. And it's a very interesting report because they were asked, why should we have ... offices of Parliament, how should we set them up? And they said everyone agreed, all six members, both sides of the House, all six members said it was to hold the executive to account because that was Parliament's responsibility.

Mm. So to some extent, leave aside what the Environment Act says- Mm... I strongly agree with that. I think that this office, and in a different way, the other two offices of Parliament, are here to help Parliament hold the executive to account. Because in our system, the executive's got all the keys and just about all the power.

Yeah. So if you want to have a high quality discussion about what's being proposed, this is a safe, reliable source of information- Mm. Yep... that you can look to. You ask, what's a better way? I'm sorry, if you're unhappy, you say, "Couldn't make sense of that."

I'm not unhappy. I'm just wondering. No, but if your view is you're not doing it the most useful way, you need to say so. Otherwise, I would strongly recommend you read what we do, come and talk to us, and make use of it. Can I just add- Which I do. I think the biggest change in the way we operate over the last few years is not writing summaries.

It's that resource shift that the commissioner outlined early on in that presentation. Yeah. Quite a big resource has shifted from long-term proactive investigations to a more reactive role, based on- Yeah, and that was my next question. Yeah. Right. Is that sort of legislative advice that you give, is that included in the proportion of recommendations adopted?

No. Yeah, so that's a difficult thing to- Not as far as I'm aware... well, one thing we are looking at is having a different target for that, because- Yeah... the take-up of those recommendations is quite different. As the commissioner said, the shelf life of the big investigations is much longer, and they actually, when you look at them over a 10-year period, they have quite a high hit rate.

But as you can imagine, the shelf life of an input into the policy process is much shorter- Weeks. Weeks... and has a much lower take-up rate. When you think of the number of recs we've given you on these two bills- When it comes back to the House, either they'll have been accepted or they won't have been, and that's that.

Mm. And there's no chance to change that. Whereas, when we do a major investigation, things are alive. They're slow burners in some cases. Mm. Yeah. David, question. Thank you. I'll try and be very quick because we've got such a short time. No. Number one, your office, I believe, is the most valuable resource that we have as a committee.

It's actually free. And I mean that sincerely with the fact that you can do a lot of work that helps us be informed to do the good work we do. So I thank you for that. We have that minute today. I know. But I can't help but notice- Yeah... that your spend over many years has been stagnant. Yes. Okay? And the question I have out of that is, number one, how do you decide whether you're doing enough, doing too little- Mm.

With regards to it, because I would suggest that your spend is actually not even keeping up with inflation. So what's your process? And look, it's interesting you ask that because my budget, of course, goes to the Offices of Parliament select committee, not this one. And so we have this discussion every year, and usually there's a member who says, "Look, is it enough? Is it enough?" When I first took the job on, the first question was, "Have you got enough money?" And I said, "Well, look, I haven't done the job yet. I don't know.

Let's see." That's awesome. Of course, nine years on, I can't give that answer again. But to be frank, David, my answer is this. It's how long is a piece of string? You could double the amount of money and we could easily do more. But the question I've got, I put this to the Offices of Parliament select committee every time, push it back and say, "Would you listen to me any more because I made more recommendations?" And to be frank with you, I'm not convinced that you would.

So I've come to you twice... Not to you. I've come to Parliament twice and said that we really do need to increase it. So we've broadly kept up. But the other thing is, I've got 15, 16 FTEs. Mm. They're very high quality people. I think it's some of the best quality in the public service I know of.

You try and double that, it's not easy. To get really good people, it's quite hard. I don't want to be a lackluster agency. I want to do a small amount of stuff really well. Yep. So can I- Yes, go for it. I think it's... I'll make a statement. I think you've got to see whether you're actually satisfied or dissatisfied with what you're doing.

Yes. Quite so. I think that's more of the question you ask yourself. But can I just come back to the, is it Zanchi graph? Zanchi. Zanchi. Zanchi diagram. Yeah. Zanchi. So we always look at that every year and we think this is really good, but I'm just thinking how can we go to the next step? Oh. And at the moment, it's a quantitative, it's showing us where the dollar is being spent in a space, but it's not telling us is it meeting ministers'

policy expectations. It's not telling us whether the You talked about the benefit of the likes of monetizing the ecosystem. Yeah. What would be really good for us is also the quality of the spend. So I'm just asking the question of whether there's potential to evolve what you're doing here- Yeah. Absolutely... to the next step, because that's truly getting to the nub of what we're trying to do- Absolutely. I've been-... is hold the executive to account- Yeah. Absolutely... about what they're delivering and what's the quality of their delivery.

But I've been trying to get this committee for the last three years to use exactly that. Go to the next step. If you use that diagram, have a discussion, decide what looks to be the most interesting area of spend, and then say, "That's what we want to focus on," I can then help you ask the next round of questions. And you have a power that I don't have You can say to four or five ministries, "We want answers to these questions by next Tuesday." I can't do that.

Could I be the devil's advocate here? Yeah. I would suggest that you do work, or for us to ask you to go away and do it on predator- Sure... control. Yeah. Or water quality. Or whatever. Yeah. Get down to that more granular level. What I'm saying out of your office, and I'm looking at the budget, I'm saying, "Can we do more here?" I know you say you've got, is it half an FTE that does that graph every year? Whatever it is.

Yes, yes. I'm just sort of saying- 2.4 people... keep on doing the great work you're doing. Yeah. And how can we take it to the next level? Look, I'm sure I could make the case for a bit more if the committee wanted to use us on that sort of basis more regularly. Yeah. So far, you haven't wanted to engage on that. If you want to, then-- and if you said, "Look, we want to do an investigation a year using that diagram, focus on an area, focus on an outcome," then I think we could team up and do something very useful.

It wouldn't be hugely expensive, but it would be, I'd then know that that needed to be in my base load moving forward. If you'd like to talk about this again- Yeah... been as busy with and involved with us on. Yeah. And so, it kind of does become a measure of priorities and focus- Yeah... as well. So, thank you very much for your time. Thank you for that presentation. Thank you for all the work you do for our committee. We really appreciate it. Mm.
It's really valuable. And thank you. Pleasure. Yeah. Is that okay? Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. And we're still friends with the oils. You think? Yeah,
fair.
Yeah, and in conference. And all in Wellington, so. We don't do ours for a while. We always do it late. Welcome back to the select committee this afternoon.

Sam, Nadine, and Anna. And Anne, yep. Welcome. Thank you for joining us again. So we have 30 minutes with you. So, we can hand it back over to you if there's any kind of comments that you wanted to make generally. Otherwise, we can then open it up for questions.

Well, thank you, and Kia Ora. And if you don't mind, I wouldn't mind just taking a little minute- Yes, go ahead... to start, because this is possibly the last time MFA meets with this committee. So it'd just be nice to acknowledge a couple of things. But just, yes, introducing two of my colleagues at the table, Nadine Domise and Anne Huirapa, two members of the SLT.

Yeah, so first actually, I just wanted to thank the committee. I have been coming to meet with the Environment Select Committee, whose membership of course has changed over the years, for some time. And it has at times been testing and the lines of inquiry have been challenging, as people have jobs to do.

But I just wanted to say that at no point in the numerous years I've been coming, has the committee been anything less than fully professional and respectful of officials. So I just want to thank you for that. You've been really good to work with. Secondly, I actually want to thank the team back at MFE.

There's a small number of us tend to front these sessions and speak to the work that MFE does, but we actually don't do a lot of that work. Or certainly speak for myself, we don't do a lot of that work ourselves. So yeah, I really wanted to shout out to staff at MFE, who in the last few years, quite a lot has been happening in and around our staff. And people have carried some uncertainty through all of that and,

throughout it all, it has not got in the way of the team doing a great job and always delivering what's needed to be delivered, whether that is the reform agenda of the government of the day or our statutory function. So yeah, special shout-out to the staff. And I've had the pleasure of working with them for seven years, and I can confidently say that the culture and professional ethic and expertise that those staff are carrying into MCERT will be a huge asset for that organization.

Thank you. Thank you, Sam. And would like to, yeah, acknowledge you and the team and all the work. We do really appreciate it and appreciate all the times you've come before us on the select committee to talk many, many areas. But look, we will open it up for discussion.

So members, if you have any questions you would like to ask the team here in relation to both environment, MCERT, any of the issues. Obviously, we've traversed quite a few of the areas with the ministers this morning. But this is an opportunity to ask MFE any questions you would like to.

Yep. Cameron, did you have the next? There we go. Yes. Just a follow-up on this morning's conversation. You said you'd come back with more information. Yeah. So I might hand over to Anne, who will kick that off. Good afternoon. Yes, there are some unanswered questions from this morning I'm keen to pick up around, the EnviroCon investment. So the first one you asked around, is it co-investment?

It is, and the ratio is 40% government funding, 60% by EnviroCon. You asked- Is the government taking an ownership for it? Is it an investment or is it a grant? It's a grant. So the second point you had- Oh, so it's not, it's a grant. Yeah. So your second point was, is it going to be paid back?

No, because it is a grant, like all of our funding under the Waste Minimization Fund is. The point around there being an existing market for concrete recycling in Tauranga is a very legitimate one, and we want to acknowledge that, and also acknowledge that the press release that came out this week spoke to concrete waste quite generally.

And it wasn't as clear as it could be about the particular type of waste within that broader concrete waste group that this investment targets. So I'd like to just provide a bit of clarity and detail around that, which I'm hoping will help. So an important element of this is it targets a particular type of concrete waste called wet-ready concrete mix.

Surplus. Yes. So it's the- The leftover... it's the leftover wet concrete that's in trucks. It is perishable, it sets quickly. It's got about a three to four-hour lifespan. It needs to be rapidly collected before it hardens. Our analysis of what's available in Tauranga indicated that there isn't currently a facility that can address that specific type of waste.

And that was confirmed by our work with Tauranga Council. It's our understanding, others may have different views, but our analysis also showed that that particular waste is going to landfill. So just a follow-up question on two parts. Sure. Well, it's probably more of a comment. I know from my own experience, maybe the council doesn't know, but there are definitely people reusing surplus overboard to make blocks. Yeah, so that is actually happening at the moment.

And I think, I can think of two companies off the top of my head. But second thing I was going to ask was a question that I had this morning for more information was there was the claim it was going to divert 28 tons, 28,000 tons. And I asked, was there a knowledge by MFE of how many tons were actually going to landfill at the moment?

Because talking to people in the construction area in Tauranga especially, saying, "That seems an awful lot, considering what's actually already being recycled." Okay. So is there a knowledge of how many ton is actually going to landfill? Can we come back to you on that number? It's quite a detailed point. Well, I- Is that all right? You can, but I'll just make the point that this morning I've asked.

You said there was a business case. I would assume something like that would be in the business case you'd come back with out the answer. So I'm happy for you to come back, but I think you have come back, and I specifically asked for that this morning, but- Well, I think that they've said they'll come back, Cameron. So, I think that's fair. Can I ask one more thing? Sure. But the business case, how public is that?

Is it in the public realm? Do we post it on our website or something like that? Well, would I be able to see it? I would expect so, just in the same way that you would under official information. We're happy to provide more details. There is quite a lot of analysis that's gone into it. We can share that. Quite happy to. Okay.

Yeah, I suspect that a lot of the documents associated with the funding bid are probably Not published proactively over the 10 month. But you could get access to them to the extent they weren't commercially sensitive, sorry, in no way out. I would have to check that, though. We can come back on that.

Yeah. So I think that you'll come back with some of that information. Thank you. Look, we'll now go to Lan, at BlueScope. Yeah. Thank you, and thank you for all the work, MFE. It's been fantastic. Yeah. My question was about this new MCERT and your move to MCERT and, because we heard a lot from ministers how this is like a new chapter and environmental aspects are going to be strengthened and whatnot. And I'm really interested because we didn't get any information from ministers about the practicalities of how MFE moving to MCERT will actually

work. So I'm specifically interested in when MCERT gives policy advice to ministers, to government, what will that look like? What is contemplated in the next while? Can you help us understand that? Specific terms. So, look, I think I can start to answer that question.

I'm not sure I can give you a definitive answer because some of the ways of working and operating are yet to be confirmed, and that'll be something for... Remembering, MCERT at the moment is a shell. Mm-hmm. And the staff haven't transferred, and it really doesn't take life until the 1st of July, and there's a heck of a lot of work in front of the SLT.

So there'll be quite a lot of thinking about how to operationalize the way we deliver advice. Having said that, I think Minister Greig mentioned that. So the Environment Act provisions and the expectations that rest on, or have rested on MFE and the Environment Act to provide advice in relation to environmental outcomes and issues, those carry through and are attached now to the Secretary's environment.

So, the secretary will have to demonstrate how they have discharged that responsibility. I imagine that the annual report will be one mechanism through which they would need to report on how they've discharged that responsibility. But there are probably a number of ways in which you could operationalize that expectation. Part of it will be at the general level, exposing the trade-offs that are associated with judgments, and in particular when we're talking about environmental or climate impacts, being able to make sure when our advice goes across that we're clear

about what the environmental or climate implications of a particular decision or set of options will be. But there are quite a few ways you could operationalize that. Part of it will be cultural. I think the culture of the place and the expectation set by leadership will be probably more important than whatever systems you build to ensure that those trade-offs are drawn out.

Mm-hmm. So I think there's a bit in statute, there'll be a lot in culture and leadership, and there'll be some of it played out through systems and practices that you design- Mm... in terms of your ways of working and operating. So just something to throw on that. So is there an intention, and maybe the CE of MCERT is the only person who can answer this, I don't know.

Yeah. But is there an intention as far as you're aware that there will be essentially, MCERT will tell the public how they are going about these things before we get to the end of the first year, and they're actually reporting how they actually did it? Because I think that's the main concern that's come from the public.

They're like, "Well, what is this actually going to look like now? Are these trade-offs going to be behind the scenes, and how are we going to know about them?" Now, I'm noting you're saying different ways to do it. Yeah. But what about the intention about actually bringing people along with the new organization? Yeah. I feel like I can't speak for Jeremy, and it'd be a bit unfair of me to try and characterize what he is anticipating at the moment, and he will still be doing his own thinking about exactly how to bring that to life.

So I suppose I can only really talk about what there is, and there's still a whole lot of statutory expectations on the secretary. There's still a whole lot of legislation that we have to administer. There's a range of acts that place obligations, Waste Minimization Act, Climate Change Response Act, amongst a number, that place obligations on government to, and that MFE will, well, MCERT will need to continue to provide support to ministers in ensuring that they do discharge those obligations.

But then there's a whole lot of design, as I said, that Jeremy needs to be given the chance to- Mm-hmm... to work through and then represent himself. What about in the transfer, like the MFE part itself, what about priorities that MFE will be bringing through into MCERT?

Like, do they retain, or is it sort of, again, up to the CE to decide what they are? So, well, again, there is a statutory architecture. It's really important to keep that in mind. So that sets some parameters, and we will have statutory functions that we need to exercise.

Of course, governments will have different priorities, and we'll have to tilt accordingly in terms of our work program. I can tell you that- We have done some business planning internally. I think it's reasonable to share that that business planning has been done on the basis of, for now, essentially extending the activities and the functions that we currently undertake.

And so we've extended out and planned to continue to deliver the kind of work that we are currently delivering, and we'll do that for six months. We've got an election, of course, in November, and so inevitably there will be new government priorities that emerge post-election, and then we'll need to make adjustments. So...

Darling. Could I? Oh, do you mind if I just maybe give a practical example of what is in the current work program and sets out the work program for this year. So we know that we, again, have a big work program ahead in terms of the national instruments. That includes the environmental limit setting framework, it includes biodiversity, it includes freshwater, it includes a number of other matters, including natural hazards, et cetera. That has not changed.

And we also equally know that we have a big work program ahead in terms of land use planning, spatial planning, standardized zones. That has not changed. So as Sam has indicated, the statutes, the key functions remain, and the job for MERT, and officials within MERT, is to provide, as always, free and frank advice on all of those matters in order to progress that work ahead and to provide that advice.

At day one, the expectation is that we will be continuing to do the same work and meet the expectations of the same statutory requirements that we currently operate to. Mm-hmm. Thank you. We will move, because we've got a couple of other primary questions. Rachel. You can... Yeah, okay. Okay, just a little one on waste, going back to the waste secretary.

Are there- Mm... Litter Act amendments as well? It's all still caught up together. Yeah. So I'm pretty confident that we were still planning to. Nothing's changed in the sense of bringing together the Litter Act and the Waste Minimization Act, and that's part of the reform agenda.

Okay. And so you don't... Can you give any more specific update, or it's just- No, it's really just a matter of timing, of finding house time- to get the bill introduced. Okay. Thank you. So it is written, drafted. I'd have to double, triple-check that.

Nearly. So we've actually, so the policy decisions have been made, and the Cabinet paper has been proactively released. So it goes through the detail of what the actual policy changes will be. process, so- Yep... yeah. I mean, what I would say, in and around that process underway is that we would expect that once the legislation is settled, if there's been any changes from introduction, then we would look at that and modeling as required.

Okay. Thank you. And I think, I mean, yeah, obviously- No, no, no. I appreciate that... we came through the submission process, so, Lan. Just on broadly, so the sort of 400 million, roughly 390 something, for RM reform, is your understanding that that in its entirety is for the functions of MERT to see that work through? Or is there areas of that funding that has been tagged, for example, for implementation and actually helping, like maybe going to local government or helping local government implement stuff? Can you help us understand that?

Absolutely. So the funding specifically for the MERT component, noting of course, that there's a portion there for the Ministry of Justice for the planning tribunal, but for the planning system, MERT component, is essentially, 294 million. I think about it roughly in terms of a third.

So a third of that is for the national instruments that I was referring to before. That is quite a big work program. A third is for supporting the change, so the transition for local government, and that specifically, we have work underway with- Local government with individual councils to understand what's most helpful for them, depending upon the final form of the legislation, of course.

And then a third is the data and the digital investment that I spoke to earlier today. Okay, thank you. That's really helpful. Yeah. Rachel. A different query. Going to the fast track, we had some conversation about that earlier. Just a question for the ministry on what work the ministry is doing to look at how that's working and what the impact of those decisions on the fast track are to other planning considerations. The minister and I had a bit of back and forth about saying it's the end of all planning in Queenstown, for instance.

So is there some analysis of what the consequences of those fast track is, and how will that impact on future planning decisions? What I would say about fast track is that when you think of it in terms of the planning system, which is where the conversation was going this morning, the fast track provides a pathway for those big complex projects and other projects.

The ministers have been really clear that that pathway is an important pathway and remains. In sitting alongside the more enabling pathways that are currently being considered in the bills for the legislation. You heard from the minister this morning, his ambition in terms of unlocking capacity and growth.

So, I won't speak further to that. What I will say is that this is about providing, in its totality, a more enabling planning system. When it comes to the individual detail of, for example, what that means on local networks, then there is flexibility both within the fast track, but within also the broader planning system for councils to have those conversations with individual developers and applicants.

So that's the policy intention there. So no is the answer there. What about any analysis into the... Something that you've all heard me go on about is that the purpose statement of the fast track doesn't have any environmental caveat to it.

Doesn't say in accordance with sustainable management or anything like that. And then there's the hierarchy and the legislation that you have to give the greatest weight, or there's some other words to the purpose, which is to facilitate projects. And has there been any analysis on the decisions that have come out on the fast track and what impact they might have on the environment that would be different from if a decision had been made under the RMA?

Fast track does enable environmental considerations to be considered. There is a different threshold in the legislation, of course. Yeah. There's the hierarchy difference. There is a hierarchy difference, but it does enable those significant environmental considerations to be considered. So, that is the policy intention.

The new legislation also is intended to be more enabling, obviously subject to any changes through the environments committee process. In terms of what that all means on achieving outcomes, the new legislation does include quite strong provisions around monitoring both the outcomes of the legislation, the system performance framework.

So, that is where the net effect, if you like, of both the promotion of growth and housing those other elements alongside environmental limits and what that means. That's where that will come together and be monitored both at a local scale, but also at a national level.

Okay, so you're saying the monitoring will happen in the new system? It will. And then, are you also part saying, I don't want to put too many words into your mouth, but that the fast track arrangements are supposed to be similar to the new systems arrangements in terms of a hierarchy and where the environment comes into it, and to be more enabling?

No, I'm not saying that specifically at all. I'm really just pointing out that there are different thresholds. But I did want to make the point that the environmental considerations are able to be considered in fast track as well, obviously within the new legislation. But you are right, in that there are different thresholds there. Thank you, and Lan will take one last question.

Just a supplementary on fast track. Have there been resourcing changes within MFE as the fast track process evolves? I think it was about five to six FTEs that were supporting the minister in her commentary on those projects. Is that sort of standard now? Is it more or less?

So there are two roles or functions that the ministry undertakes in relation to fast track. The first is with respect to stewardship of the system, and the resourcing around that team, for the 26/27 financial year sits at 13, seven of which are permanent. And then there is another area and another function that we perform to support the ministers for environment and climate change, and there are 3.6 FTEs allocated for that work.

Okay, thank you. And sorry, the stewardship, is that about Accepting the applications, checking them for completeness, so that's not the EPA that's undertaking that. That's- So there's the two pathways are the referral process and the EPA-led process, the substantive one.

MFE administers the referral process, so there is a core operational delivery role there. We also oversee the effectiveness and efficiency of the entire system. So one way to give you an example of that is, the amendment bill that went through at the end of last year that was focused on operational efficiencies and how to improve the effectiveness of the system. So that's part of our stewardship role.

Mm-hmm. We also have undertaken a review of the cost recovery system that we have in place. There was an expectation that we would do a review at the one-year mark. It's more focused, just to build on Rachel's question, it's more focused on the operational efficiency as the statute was designed. So it's not going into the broader strategic impacts of the regime per se.

Okay. Thank you. And so that stewardship- Standardly... that's not cost recoverable, that's absorbed by the ministry? That's a good question. Yeah, the cost recovery piece would be the referral process, and then that broader stewardship piece, yes, you're correct, my understanding is it's not cost recoverable.

Okay. Thank you. Look, thank you very much. We have met time today. We appreciate your time and the discussions and topics we've covered today. So thank you very much. And the ministry as a whole. Yeah. Yeah. We appreciate that. Yes, I know. We acknowledged that earlier, but a full acknowledgement to- Yeah... Sam and the team and everybody.
